question: Is the miniature city clearly positioned entirely inside the glass sphere, with no elements floating outside the glass boundary?
Is the watercolor forest rendered as a curved backing paper that visually conforms to the inner rear wall of the sphere?
Is the miniature city visually positioned in front of the watercolor backing paper (correct occlusion)?
Do the city structures near the edges of the sphere exhibit pronounced refractive magnification and curved distortion?
Are the studio specular highlight reflections from the original glass sphere preserved on the surface, overlaying the internal contents?
Is there visible depth-of-field layering, with the city or foreground elements appearing sharper than the background backing paper?
Is the paper texture of the watercolor backing subtly visible without overpowering the imagery?
Are the distinct building structures (skyscrapers, modern architecture) from the input city image recognizable inside the sphere?
Is the green watercolor style and forest silhouette from the second input image recognizable in the backing layer?
Is the crystal square base from the original glass sphere image present and properly supporting the sphere?
Does the glass sphere retain its transparency, allowing clear visibility of the internal city and backing paper?
Does the overall image retain the photorealistic studio photography aesthetic of the original glass sphere input?
